## Directions

[You can download the `.qmd` file for this lab here.](https://github.com/nwakim/BSTA_513_26S/blob/main/project/LastName_FirstInit_Lab_01.qmd)

The above link will take you to your **editing** file. **Please do not remove anything from this editing file!!** You will only add your code and work to this file.

## Lab activities

### Filtering to Your Target Subpopulation

To implement an asset-focused analysis, we must first isolate the subpopulation relevant to your scope using `filter()`.

* *If Option 1 or 3 (Food/Housing Safety Net):* Filter your data to households navigating budget constraints, defined as those living below 200% of the Federal Poverty Level (`FPL_LT50 == 1 | FPL50_100 == 1 | FPL100_150 == 1 | FPL150_200 == 1`).
* *If Option 2 (Healthcare Access):* Filter to individuals relying on public coverage options.
* *If Option 4 (Work Support):* Filter to active working families or households with children (`NUM_CHILD_HH >= 1`).

::: callout-important

#### Task

Write a pipeline that filters the dataset down to your specific population of interest. Save this as a new object called `wbns_project_sub`. Print out the number of rows before and after filtering to verify the operation.
:::

### Selecting Project Variables

Your dataset contains hundreds of variables. For project clarity, reduce your data frame to only columns essential for your scope.

::: callout-important

#### Task

Use `select()` to keep only the following columns in your subset data frame: `ID`, your demographic grouping variables (e.g., `PPETHM`, `PPGENDER`, or `PPAGE`), your programmatic support variables, and your outcome variable (e.g., `Q23`).
:::

### Constructing the Combined Asset Index

Your project must create a brand-new variable that combines information from at least two source columns using `mutate()`. For example, in Option 1, you can add up program binary metrics to calculate a total safety net index.

Use `if_else()` or `case_when()` inside your mutate pipeline to catch and handle missing values or refusals, converting them to `0` or `NA` so they do not distort your math.

::: callout-important

#### Task

Create your new combined asset metric using `mutate()`. Write a brief paragraph explaining:

1. Which source variables you chose.
2. The code logic you applied to handle missing values or refusals.
3. What a high score vs. low score means on your new metric.
:::

### Inverting a Deficit Variable into an Asset Lens

Many survey questions are framed around deficits (e.g., `Q23` asks about eating less due to lack of money). To fulfill our asset-based goal, we will use `case_when()` to invert this into a positive framing. For example, a response of "No" to `Q23` means a family maintained full dietary stability.

::: callout-important

#### Task

Use `mutate()` and `case_when()` to transform your primary health/well-being outcome variable into an asset-framed factor variable. Use `glimpse()` to display your final, transformed data frame.
:::
